3 min read
Just now

--

A view from Kampala on why the continent must contribute and how
to start today.

Something Bothers Me.
Every AI assistant I use has been trained mostly on English text from the internet. It knows
about New York, London, and Tokyo in extraordinary detail. Ask it about Owino Market, Nakasero market, Kiboga district or
Makerere Hill, or what it means to pay school fees in Uganda it gives you a generic answer. A technically correct, culturally hollow answer. That is not the AI’s fault. It is the fault of who has been doing the contributing. And that is a problem we can fix.

language model training data geographic distributionThe Real Gap — It Is Not Access, It Is Contribution
People talk about the African AI gap in terms of access not enough GPUs, not enough internet, not enough funding. All of that is real. But there is a subtler gap that gets less attention: the gap in contribution.
Open-source AI is built by whoever shows up. Hugging Face has over 500,000 models. GitHub has millions of AI repositories. The people who contribute to these who write the datasets, who file the pull requests, who build the evaluation benchmarks shape what these tools are good at. Right now, that is overwhelmingly a Western and East Asian group.
https://huggingface.co

What Uganda Specifically Has to Offer
Uganda has 43 distinct languages. It has unique agricultural, health, and governance challenges. It has a young, fast-growing tech community centred around Kampala. These are not disadvantages in the AI space they are datasets waiting to be built.
Luganda has over 6 million speakers. Runyankole has 2-3 million. Acholi is spoken by
hundreds of thousands. None of these languages have high-quality AI training data. The
person who builds that data is doing pioneering work, work that will be cited, built upon, and funded.

Uganda language mapHow to Start Contributing This Week
1. Collect 500 sentence pairs in your language and upload them to Hugging Face
Datasets.
2. Join the Masakhane community they run regular workshops and have active Slack and Discord channels.

3. Start translating AI documentation many people are blocked not by knowledge but by language.
4. File issues or small bug fixes on open-source AI tools being a contributor on major repos is a real credential. You do not need to build AGI. You need to build one useful thing, document it well, and share it openly. That is how open source works. That is how reputations are built.

End of Article #2 | Tags: Open Source, Africa, AI, Computer Science, Diversity
